bibliographic.citation: Geoffroy de La Tour-Landry, The Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry, ed. Thomas Wright, rev. ed., EETS Original Series 33 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1906).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 33
bibliographic.title: The Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry

entities.description: The immediate print source. It retains Wright's 1868 composite edition, incorporates Brock's corrections, and adds renewed collation and paratext.
entities.label: Wright's 1906 revised EETS edition
notes: Rossiter copied Harley MS 1764 and inserted missing passages from Caxton.
Brock compiled a thirteen-page Additions and Corrections list for errors in the first edition.
Fox checked new proofs through p. 48 against Harley MS 1764; Furnivall checked the remainder, added French-source notes, and wrote the foreword.
Munro supplied the contents, fresh notes, glossary, phrases and proverbs, and subject index. The U-M encoding omits the contents and back matter.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1906 and is outside United States copyright term.
rights.component: The 1906 edition's editorial text and paratext
notes: This conclusion concerns the historical edition, not necessarily the hosted scan files or provider metadata.

bibliographic.citation: Geoffroy de La Tour-Landry, The Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry, ed. Thomas Wright, EETS Original Series 33 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1868).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 33
bibliographic.title: The Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry

entities.description: The first printed edition of the anonymous translation, based on Rossiter's transcript and collation of Harley MS 1764 with Caxton supplies where Harley is defective.
entities.label: Wright's 1868 first EETS edition

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1868 and is outside United States copyright term.
rights.component: The 1868 edition's editorial text and paratext

entities.description: The only known surviving copy of the anonymous earlier Middle English translation, at ff. 1r-54v, with losses after fols. 24 and 32 and an incomplete ending.

holding.notes: Consultation is in London; the catalog says a letter of introduction is required.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 1764

physicalDescription.dimensions: 315 x 215 mm; text space 220 x 160 mm
physicalDescription.extent: ff. 1* + 54, plus three unfoliated modern paper flyleaves at the beginning and end
physicalDescription.format: Codex, mostly gatherings of eight, written in two columns
notes: Gothic cursive script; decorated initials and one rubbed historiated initial on f. 1r.
The manuscript lacks leaves after fols. 24 and 32 and is incomplete at the end.

reviewNotes: The target work is the anonymous earlier Middle English translation. Geoffroy authored the French source, and Caxton translated a separate later English version.
Harley MS 1764 lacks leaves after fols. 24 and 32 and ends imperfectly at fol. 54v. Caxton passages in Wright are substitutes, not reconstructed anonymous wording.
The large Caxton-derived spans are printed pp. 72-75, 99-101, and 167-205; a small opening repair on p. 1 is also marked.
CERL's legacy digital link concerns the parallel copy C.11.c.6 = IB.55086, not Wright's IB.55085, and was unavailable when checked.
Michels-Kohlhage reports EEBO images from IB.55085. The subscription record was not directly authenticated, so it is modeled at medium confidence.

The Harley ZIP contains 127 provider-native full images. Although 1800 pixels wide was requested, the helper avoided upscaling and retrieved full images generally about 2000 pixels high with variable width.
The XML header says front matter and notes were omitted, but the file preserves a title page, two front divisions, and 102 NOTE1 elements. Only the observed omissions are asserted.
The CME repository Unlicense applies to contributor-controlled repository material; it does not automatically override inherited U-M, British Library, or Internet Archive terms.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of a 1997 University of Michigan encoding made from Thomas Wright's 1906 revised EETS edition. Wright's text is composite: it principally transcribes British Library Harley MS 1764, the only known witness to an anonymous earlier Middle English translation, but uses William Caxton's distinct later translation for a small opening repair, two internal lacunae, and the entire missing ending. Matching Thomas Lane ownership inscriptions printed by Wright with Seymour de Ricci's census identifies British Library IB.55085 as the Caxton copy used. The U-M encoding preserves the edition title page, Furnivall foreword, Wright introduction, pp. 1-205, and many footnotes, while omitting the contents and back matter. Complete public scans of both Wright editions and a complete 127-canvas British Library IIIF facsimile of Harley MS 1764 are cached privately. The IIIF manifest asserts that its images are public domain in most countries other than the United Kingdom; its linked reuse page currently returns 404, so UK status and any provider permission requirements remain unresolved rather than overriding that non-UK assertion.

catalogSummary.title: The Knight of the Tower

catalogingSubject.label: The Knight of the Tower
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged work is the anonymous earlier Middle English prose translation of Geoffroy IV de La Tour-Landry's French Livre pour l'enseignement de ses filles, known only from British Library Harley MS 1764. It is not Geoffroy's French work, William Caxton's separate 1483 translation printed in 1484, Harley MS 1764 as a physical object, Thomas Wright's composite 1868/1906 edition, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML. Because Harley 1764 has two internal lacunae and lacks its ending, Wright's edition and the electronic text insert passages from Caxton's distinct translation; those supplied passages are edition-level content and are not silently attributed to the anonymous translator.

contentParts.label: Furnivall's 1906 foreword
notes: Modern edition paratext, not part of the medieval work.
contentParts.summary: A modern foreword explaining Rossiter's transcription, Caxton supplies, Brock's corrections, the 1906 proofing and revision, and Munro's added back matter.

contentParts.label: Wright's historical and textual introduction
notes: Modern edition paratext, not part of the medieval work.
contentParts.summary: A modern introduction on Geoffroy, the French work, its translations, the anonymous earlier English version, Harley MS 1764, and Wright's decision to fill manuscript defects from Caxton.

contentParts.label: Anonymous earlier Middle English translation

contentParts.label: Passages supplied from Caxton's separate translation

notes: The long final supply includes Caxton's 1483 translation and 1484 printing colophon; it is not a recovered ending of the anonymous translation.
The exact physical copy is identified as British Library IB.55085 by the ownership inscriptions reproduced in Wright's edition and matched in de Ricci's census.
contentParts.summary: Four edition repairs: a small bracketed opening supply where Harley fol. 1r is difficult to read; the missing leaf after fol. 24v; the missing material after fol. 32v; and the complete continuation after Harley ends at fol. 54v, including Caxton's colophon.

notes: The Middle English Dictionary conventionally dates the text ?c1450. No normalized range is imposed because the witness's 1425-1474 production range cannot supply a logical lower bound for the earlier act of translation.
This dates production of the extant manuscript, not necessarily the act of translation.

dateStatements.display: First printed in Thomas Wright's EETS edition in 1868
notes: The first edition was composite where Harley lacked text; the anonymous translation itself had not previously been printed.

extent.description: The manifest counts one intellectual prose work. Structurally, the XML has 135 DIV1 elements: two modern front-matter divisions and 133 body divisions comprising one prologue, 131 chapter containers, and one Caxton colophon. The edition's headings reach Chapter CXLIV, but combined headings, omitted chapter numbers, a duplicated Chapter CXX label, and the composite witness repairs mean that 144 is an editorial numbering endpoint rather than 144 independent works or one-to-one XML containers. The file also contains six DIV2, 186 P, 218 PB, 259 MILESTONE, and 102 NOTE1 elements.

formStatements.description: A prose translation organized as a prologue and moral exempla. The prologue explicitly says that the book was set in prose rather than rhyme.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: French and Latin in the XML occur in Wright's modern introduction and notes. They are edition-layer quotation languages, not additional discovery languages of the anonymous Middle English work.
The current British Library record supersedes Wright's hand-based claim that the manuscript itself proves a Henry VI-reign translation date; manuscript production and translation are kept distinct.

Wright's exact Caxton source copy is identified as British Library IB.55085 by matching the Thomas Lane inscriptions printed in the edition to de Ricci's copy census. This is strong copy-level evidence, not a claim that every surviving 1484 copy has identical annotations.
Edition, encoding, facsimile access, reproduction requests, and rights evidence are maintained in manifests/lineage/works/KntTour-L.json.

next_steps: Consult a modern critical edition or dedicated study of the earlier anonymous translation and distinguish linguistic localization from Harley MS 1764's production place.
openQuestions.question: Can a modern linguistic or textual study narrow the anonymous translation's date, dialect, or place of composition beyond the conventional ?c1450 and broad English origin?

next_steps: Consult source studies comparing the anonymous translation with the French manuscript tradition.
Do not identify Royal MS 19 C VII as the translator's exemplar without direct evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which French manuscript or textual branch did the anonymous translator use?

next_steps: Treat the Caxton continuation as editorial substitution, not reconstruction of the anonymous wording.
Review any modern comparative edition for recoverable indirect evidence.
openQuestions.question: How closely did the lost ending of the anonymous translation correspond to Caxton's supplied ending?

notes: The English translation and the English production of its sole witness support a broad English cultural origin. No reviewed evidence establishes an exact place of translation.

scope.description: The earlier Middle English translation as preserved incompletely in Harley MS 1764

notes: This responsibility belongs only to the supplied edition passages, not to the cataloged anonymous translation as a whole.

scope.description: Passages imported into Wright's composite edition where Harley MS 1764 is illegible, lacunose, or truncated
The 1868 edition and the inherited editorial text revised in 1906
Oversight, proof correction, additional notes, and foreword for the 1906 revised edition

summaries.value: An anonymous fifteenth-century English translation of Geoffroy IV de La Tour-Landry's instructional book for his daughters. A father frames a long series of biblical, legendary, historical, and social exempla as counsel on virtue and vice, worship, reputation, dress, speech, courtship, marriage, household behavior, and the dangers of misconduct. The work is prose and is directed especially toward the moral and social education of young gentlewomen.

Harley MS 1764 is the only known surviving copy of this earlier translation. It lacks leaves after fols. 24 and 32 and ends imperfectly at fol. 54v. Wright filled those gaps and the missing ending from Caxton's separate translation, so the 1868/1906 edition and KntTour-L XML are composite representations rather than complete transcriptions of the anonymous translation.

The XML preserves the revised edition's title page, Furnivall foreword, Wright introduction, medieval text on printed pp. 1-205, and 102 NOTE1 footnotes. It omits the printed table of contents and the back matter credited to Jack Munro: additional notes, glossary, phrases and proverbs, and subject index. The header sentence saying that front matter and notes were not included is therefore not an accurate description of the file as preserved.

notes: The current British Library catalog uses this concise title for the anonymous earlier translation.
Wright's edition title. The carrier also includes passages from Caxton's separate translation, so the title alone does not define a homogeneous witness text.
titles.value: The Book of the Knight of La Tour-Landry

notes: Legacy U-M uniform title preserving the relationship to the French source. It does not distinguish this anonymous translation from Caxton's later English version.
